Major Political Periods.description: Fall of the Ancien Régime, Declaration of the Rights of Man, the Terror and Directory.
Napoleon Bonaparte's European conquests, the Civil Code, reorganisation of Europe.
Return of monarchies, rise of nationalism, revolutionary waves of 1830 and 1848.
Italian and German unifications, emergence of modern nation-states.
Industrial boom, colonial imperialism, technological optimism before the Great War.
Global economic crisis, rise of totalitarianism, political instability.
Ideological confrontation between the United States and the USSR; arms race and space race.
Independence movements in Asia, Africa and Oceania after the Second World War.
Worldwide integration of economies, cultures and technologies after the end of the Cold War.

Wars & Conflicts.description: Napoleon's military campaigns across Europe, from Spain to Russia.
France, the United Kingdom and the Ottoman Empire against Russia; one of the first mediatised wars.
Conflict between Northern and Southern states over slavery and national unity.
French defeat, German unification, Paris Commune.
20 million deaths; collapse of the Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, Russian and German empires.
Conflict between republicans and Franco's nationalists; first mass use of Nazi air warfare, direct prelude to World War II.
70 to 85 million deaths; genocide of European Jews, Japan's surrender.
Conflict between France and the Việt Minh; defeat at Diên Biên Phu, Vietnamese independence.
Conflict between North Korea (and China) and South Korea (and the UN); armistice, partition.
Conflict between France and the FLN; Algerian independence in 1962.
American involvement; fall of Saigon in 1975, Vietnamese reunification.
Border and ideological conflict between Khomeinist Iran and Saddam Hussein's Iraq.
International coalition against Iraq following the invasion of Kuwait; liberation of Kuwait.
Breakup of Yugoslavia; conflicts in Croatia, Bosnia, Kosovo; ethnic cleansing.
American and NATO intervention after 9/11; withdrawal in 2021.
Large-scale Russian invasion; largest war in Europe since 1945.

Society & Economy.description: Mechanisation, railways, mass urbanisation, transformation of societies.
Social struggles, birth of socialism, trade unions and workers' parties.
Partition of the world between European powers; exploitation of colonised peoples.
Global economic collapse triggered by the 1929 crash; mass unemployment, rise of populism.
Exceptional economic growth in Western Europe; full employment, welfare state, consumer society.
From the ECSC to the Treaty of Rome and then the European Union.
Growing awareness of climate change, Paris Agreement, energy transition.

Technological Revolutions.description: Steam engine, railways, steelmaking — drivers of the first industrialisation.
Electrification of cities, telegraph, telephone, radio, electric motors.

Technological Revolutions.titre: Age of Oil and the Automobile
Technological Revolutions.description: Internal combustion engine, automobile, aircraft, mass production.
Nuclear fission, atomic bombs, nuclear power plants, arms race.
Satellites, first humans in space, planetary probes, International Space Station.
Personal computers, the Internet, mobile phones, social networks.
Machine learning, large language models, generative AI.

Technology & Innovation.description: Decisive improvement of the steam engine — driving force of the Industrial Revolution.
Alessandro Volta invents the first source of direct electric current — makes electricity usable and paves the way for all modern electrical engineering.
Start of the railway age — transformation of trade and territory.
Michael Faraday discovers that a changing magnetic field produces an electric current — founding principle of all generators and alternators.
Samuel Morse connects Baltimore to Washington — first long-distance electrical communication, forerunner of the telephone and the Internet.
Jean-Joseph Étienne Lenoir designs the first gas combustion engine — precursor of the petrol engine.
Link between the Mediterranean and the Red Sea — revolution in world trade.
Transmission of voice over distance — revolution in communications.
Incandescent lighting — beginning of the electrification of cities.
First car with an internal combustion engine — start of the automotive age.
Wireless transmission — birth of mass media.
First controlled flight — start of the aeronautical age.
Democratisation of the automobile through the assembly line.
First televised images — future mass medium for information and culture.
The BBC launches the world's first regular television service from Alexandra Palace.
Building block of modern electronics and computing.
First decentralised computer network — precursor to the Internet.
Computer on a chip — beginning of the personal computing age.
First mobile phone call — start of mobile telephony.
First supersonic transatlantic commercial flight between Paris and New York — symbol of Franco-British technological prestige.
Birth of the web — revolution in access to information and global communications.
Revolution in mobile computing and digital usage.
ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude — artificial intelligence transforms work and creativity.

Politics & Diplomacy.description: First modern state founded on natural rights and popular sovereignty.
Overthrow of absolute monarchy, Declaration of the Rights of Man, birth of the Republic.
King of France guillotined in Paris — symbolic break with the Ancien Régime.
Napoleon crowns himself Emperor of the French at Notre-Dame de Paris.
First Black republic and first nation to abolish slavery, born of a revolution.
Liberal insurrectionary wave in France, Belgium and Poland.

Politics & Diplomacy.titre: Springtime of Nations and Communist Manifesto
Politics & Diplomacy.description: Liberal revolutions across Europe; publication of the Manifesto by Marx and Engels.

Politics & Diplomacy.titre: German Unification and the Paris Commune
Politics & Diplomacy.description: Proclamation of the German Empire at Versailles; workers' uprising in Paris crushed.
Partition of Africa between European powers without consultation of its peoples.
Bloody Sunday in Saint Petersburg — first revolutionary tremor in Tsarist Russia.
Lenin seizes power — birth of the USSR.
Redraws the map of Europe; conditions imposed on Germany, seeds of the next war.

Politics & Diplomacy.titre: Hitler as Chancellor — Start of the Third Reich
Politics & Diplomacy.description: Nazis come to power in Germany.
United Nations — foundation of the post-war international order.
End of British rule — largest decolonisation of the 20th century.
29 African and Asian countries assert their neutrality — birth of the Non-Aligned Movement.
US President assassinated in Dallas, 22 November 1963.
End of Germany's division — collapse of the Eastern Bloc.
End of the Soviet Union — end of the Cold War and global geopolitical reshaping.
800,000 Tutsis massacred in 100 days by Hutu militias — failure of the international community to intervene.

Politics & Diplomacy.titre: Election of Nelson Mandela — End of Apartheid
Politics & Diplomacy.description: First Black President of South Africa — culmination of 40 years of struggle against institutionalised racial segregation.

Politics & Diplomacy.titre: Handover of Hong Kong to China
Politics & Diplomacy.description: The United Kingdom returns Hong Kong to China on 1 July — end of 156 years of British colonial rule.
Wave of popular revolutions across the Arab world (Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, Syria…).
The United Kingdom votes to leave the European Union.

Medicine & Health.description: First successful vaccination — foundation of preventive medicine.
Louis Pasteur demonstrates that infectious diseases are caused by micro-organisms — revolution that founds modern medicine and hygiene.
H1N1 flu pandemic; 50 to 100 million deaths worldwide — more deadly than World War I. Mainly affected 20–40 year-olds.
First insulin injection in a diabetic patient — medicine's victory over a deadly disease.
Discovery of the first antibiotic — revolution in medicine.
First approval in the United States — social revolution and women's emancipation.
Prof. Barnard performs the first heart transplant in Cape Town — revolution in cardiac surgery.
First medical image by magnetic resonance — revolution in non-invasive diagnostic imaging.
The WHO certifies the complete eradication of smallpox — first and only extinction of a human infectious disease in history, achieved through vaccination.
Discovery of the human immunodeficiency virus — global pandemic.
Global SARS-CoV-2 pandemic — widespread lockdowns, over 7 million deaths.

Wars & Conflicts.description: Napoleon's final defeat; restoration of monarchical order in Europe.

Wars & Conflicts.titre: Start of the American Civil War
Wars & Conflicts.description: Armed conflict in the United States over slavery and national unity.

Wars & Conflicts.titre: Armistice — End of World War I
Wars & Conflicts.description: 11 November 1918; approximately 20 million deaths.
Operation Overlord, 6 June 1944 — decisive turning point of the war in Europe.

Wars & Conflicts.titre: Atomic Bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki
Wars & Conflicts.description: First use of nuclear weapons in warfare; approximately 200,000 deaths.
Terrorist attacks in the United States — start of the War on Terror.
Anglo-American coalition overthrows Saddam Hussein — destabilisation of the Middle East.
Largest war in Europe since 1945.
Hamas attack on 7 October 2023 and Israeli offensive in Gaza — worst humanitarian crisis in the Middle East in decades.

Science & Exploration.description: Theory of evolution by natural selection — scientific and philosophical revolution.
Revolution in modern physics; E = mc².
First controlled chain reaction — foundation of nuclear energy and weaponry.
Discovery of the double helix — foundation of molecular biology and genetics.
USSR, 4 October 1957 — start of the Space Age.

Science & Exploration.titre: Yuri Gagarin — First Human in Space
Science & Exploration.description: USSR, 12 April 1961 — first human being in orbit.
Neil Armstrong steps onto the Moon, 21 July 1969.
Launched by NASA in April 1990 — revolution in observational astronomy.

Science & Exploration.titre: Discovery of the First Exoplanet (51 Pegasi b)
Science & Exploration.description: Mayor & Queloz (Geneva) — first confirmed planet around a Sun-like star.

Science & Exploration.titre: Dolly the Sheep — First Cloned Mammal
Science & Exploration.description: Roslin Institute (Scotland) — first cloning by somatic cell nuclear transfer, revolution in biology.
Completion of the Human Genome Project — complete mapping of the ~3 billion base pairs of human DNA; revolution in medicine and biology.
Precise gene editing — revolution in biology and medicine.

Science & Exploration.titre: First Image of a Black Hole (EHT)
Science & Exploration.description: Event Horizon Telescope captures the first direct image of a black hole (M87*) — feat of global radio astronomy.

Society & Rights.titre: Abolition of Slavery in the United States

Society & Rights.description: First country in the world to grant universal women's suffrage — start of a movement that spread across Europe and the Americas in the 20th century.
Collapse of financial markets — start of the Great Depression.

Society & Rights.titre: 19th Amendment — Women's Suffrage in the United States
Society & Rights.description: American women gain the right to vote after decades of suffragist activism.

Society & Rights.titre: Women's Right to Vote in France
Society & Rights.description: Ordinance of the French Committee of National Liberation — French women vote for the first time in April 1945.
Adopted by the UN; proclamation of the State of Israel in the same year.
Prohibition of racial segregation — victory of the civil rights movement.
Leader of the American civil rights movement assassinated in Memphis on 4 April 1968.
General strike and student revolt in France; worldwide wave of protest against the established order.

Society & Rights.titre: Oil Shock and End of the Thirty Glorious Years
Society & Rights.description: Arab oil embargo — global economic crisis, end of post-war growth.
Collapse of financial markets following the US subprime crisis.

Natural & Industrial Disasters.description: Magnitude ~8.7; 30,000 to 60,000 deaths. Followed by a tsunami and fires destroying 85% of the city; shook Enlightenment optimism.
Largest volcanic eruption in modern history (~71,000 deaths). Caused the 'Year Without a Summer' of 1816 and famines across several continents.
~36,000 deaths from the eruption and resulting tsunami. Global climate cooling and vivid red sunsets observed worldwide.
Pyroclastic flow destroyed Saint-Pierre in minutes; ~30,000 deaths. One of the worst volcanic disasters of the 20th century.
Magnitude 7.1; 75,000 to 200,000 deaths. The city was razed in under 40 seconds, followed by a tsunami.
Over 1,500 deaths in the North Atlantic — symbol of the limits of technological confidence in the Belle Époque.
Magnitude 7.9; ~142,000 deaths in Tokyo and Yokohama, massive fires and tsunami.
Flooding of the Yangtze and Huai rivers; between 1 and 4 million deaths. One of the greatest natural disasters in history.
~500,000 deaths in the Ganges–Brahmaputra delta — one of the deadliest cyclones ever recorded.
Magnitude 7.8; 240,000 to 655,000 deaths. Industrial city razed in 15 seconds; disaster concealed by Mao's regime.
Methyl isocyanate leak from the Union Carbide plant; 3,500 to 15,000 immediate deaths, hundreds of thousands chronically affected. Worst industrial accident in history.
Explosion of reactor No. 4; radioactive contamination across Europe. Accelerated the collapse of the USSR and awareness of nuclear power risks.
Magnitude 9.1 earthquake off Sumatra; ~230,000 deaths in 14 countries. Revealed the absence of a warning system in the Indian Ocean.
Magnitude 7.0; ~220,000 deaths, 1.5 million homeless. Major humanitarian catastrophe in the poorest country in the Americas.

Natural & Industrial Disasters.titre: Deepwater Horizon Oil Spill (Gulf of Mexico)
Natural & Industrial Disasters.description: BP platform explosion; 11 deaths and ~5 million barrels spilled — largest accidental oil spill in history.
Magnitude 9.1 off Tōhoku; ~20,000 deaths, partial meltdown of 3 Fukushima reactors. Triggered a global review of nuclear energy policy.
